video_title: The Perfect Neighbor | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
I don't understand why this has to be about race.
Whether the victim was black, white, purple or pink, the fact that those kids went out of their way to trespass & leave their personal belongings on someone else's property to escalate an ongoing feud with a clearly mentally unstable person, is just asking for trouble.
No matter how big of a Karen that woman was, they nonetheless kept provoking her until she finally snapped.
Murders are never justified, but the lack of self-awareness & pack mentality of the rest of the neighborhood is what caused this tragedy... and THAT should be the primary focus of this documentary to remind people that tragedies like this can be prevented.

video_title: The Perfect Neighbor | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: This documentary was interesting and unfortunate for everyone. I could see both sides of the story here. Being shot entirely on body cam footage means there's no context before the police arrived so we didn't really get the entire story at all. All of the people involved were terrible neighbors, regardless of the documentary's blatant attempt to skew the bias toward the families with very clever editing and making sure that certain "sad" clips made it in to massage people's emotions in exactly the way the film producers wanted. The people who couldn't control their children, the children who mercilessly targeted her, and the old lady who should have moved to a better area were all terrible neighbors. There's a lesson to be learned here that both parties should have talked calmly together to find a solution that worked for everyone and maybe don't go banging on someone's door that you know has a gun and tells you to go away.

video_title: The Perfect Neighbor | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
I live in a neighborhood where kids are always outside playing — sometimes even near my car — and it genuinely brings a smile to my face every time. Their laughter and energy keep the community alive and remind us what childhood is meant to be — carefree, joyful, and full of imagination.

I just watched The Perfect Neighbor on Netflix, and that woman hated kids for no reason — the complete opposite of how I feel. If I had the money and resources, I would’ve bought that miserable house she lived in, torn it down, and built a playground in its place — a space dedicated to the children, and in honor of the mother who lost her life in the story.

Who knows… maybe one day we can actually make it happen. If we started a GoFundMe and the house includes the land, that would make the dream even more possible. Kids deserve happiness, safety, and a place to just be kids — and a playground like that would give them exactly that.

video_title: The Perfect Neighbor | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
I just watched it and agree with what was said at the end. From the movie, I understood that the line explained how she was a “Karen” in the full sense. But I also want to mention that it didn’t come out of nowhere — for two years, kids and neighbors were teasing and provoking her, which really affected her not stable mental health. Technically, the neighbors and kids didn’t do anything illegal. And I don't blame them. Kids have a right to be kids, no doubts. But each action has a consequence.

My mom always warned me to stay away from people who seem sick, unstable, or mentally unhealthy — and this movie perfectly showed why she’s right. The best fight is the one that never happens.

The police were very professional and reasonable, at least based on what was shown in the movie. They warned both sides to stay away from each other multiple times.

I want to end this review with an old saying:
Sign on a graveyard — “Here lies a pedestrian. He was right.”

video_title: The Perfect Neighbor | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: Of course, it’s not right to pull the trigger in that situation, but I can understand how things unfolded. Susan put efforts into buying the property, which made her reluctant to move, while many of the people renting nearby have several kids and therefore don’t complain about the noise their children make in the neighborhood — effectively putting them all on the same side. I also noticed that the kids were leaving things around Susan’s place, which suggests they were playing quite close to her property — sometimes even late in the evening. The police also sympathized with the idea that kids need space to play and that it’s okay for them to make some noise. I’m not trying to justify her actions, but we need to understand what triggered the issue in the first place.

Spoiler:: long winded, loopy comment. 
I’ve heard the saying “you can’t fix stupid”. Sadly, in many cases, you can’t fix ignorant you can’t fix miserable.  She truly believes her explanation and her actions are appropriate and rational and all would agree.  She states that the woman banged on her door and told her to come out and talk. She pondered why this woman, whose children she’s been aggressive towards, would come and want to talk to her. Why didn’t she just call the police? Misuse 911 like she does? She’s has never considered adults having calm, rational discussion and maybe reaching a compromise, as she didn’t do it. She went straight to anger, aggression and 911. . From the start she chose to look at something that was no big deal or irritating to her but part of life into a major issue, an intentional and malicious assault. Normal occurrences and actions, in her mind, were rude and inappropriate. She explains how dramatic and traumatic it was as this woman (think in her mind she thinking different eyed) was pounding and pounding on her door and she had nowhere to go. She’s inside her own apartment and safe, no where to go no t other possible action but the one she chose. When they told her she’s going to jail, typical of her and her belief that the whole world revolves around her and what she says goes, no matter the situation.  She’s not going 😆.  I understand she must be freaking out because not for one minute thought that the outcome would be other than the one she dictated and thought of jail is terrifying (I’ll give her that).

comment_text: I watched this trial. There are very few people that cause me to have such strong, negative emotions for them, but she is one of. At first I wanted to feel compassion and feel sorry for her because you really have to be a miserable, unhappy person to behave like this, but very quickly that compassion is gone. She creates issues.  She lies. Not only does she lie about what the children have done she keeps lying and saying they are trespassing when they’re not, then you have the lies to the police officer that came to talk to her after she rammed through the fence.😢 when he comes, she doesn’t want to talk, but then she says I got locked in. Lies lies lies. she uses vulgar language around the children.  I don’t know if she’s delusional or just narcissistic because somehow she thinks that she’s the person everyone should cater to. comment_text:
Why you would let your children repeatedly torment a woman who has proven herself to be wildly unstable?  It was obvious they were driving that lady over the edge.  And it's not cheap to move, nor is it acceptable to run people out of their home by being loud, disrespectful, messy and rude.  I'm terribly sorry those children lost their mother.  But that whole neighborhood bullied an unstable lady when they could have been respectful, given her space and everyone would still be alive.  The kids were so brazen they were knocking on the cruiser window and interrogating her after the lady was arrested.  I realize I'm in the minority here, but, I'm appalled that the parents just allowed their kids to treat an old crazy lady like bullies targeting the outcast on the schoolyard.  She had nowhere to run; she was at home.  

You back a wild animal into a corner and you're probably going to get attacked.  SMH that those parents encouraged this behavior from the kids instead of respecting the needs of another rent-paying neighbor... there was PLENTY of grass for the kids to play in, literally, anywhere else!  In hindsight, I'm certain the kids wish the ADULTS told them to go play somewhere else.

Oh, and the crazy lady was correct; ALL tenants are entitled to "peaceful enjoyment," of their rental unit (it's written into the lease).  So, the police could have taken legal action against all those parents for allowing their kids to be a nuisance.  Instead, they repeatedly tormented a crazy lady and, not surprisingly, they got a crazy response.

video_title: The Perfect Neighbor | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
Although the event described in the documentary seems perfectly aligned with the controversial concepts in American criminal law of the "Castle Doctrine" and "Stand Your Ground," the outcome of the trial determined that it was not considered self-defense.

In a similar case (James Douglas Rayl) a young man named James shows up at his girlfriend's family's door. The father fires 3-4 shots through the door. The young man is hit by the first bullet and, while turning around and fleeing, is shot twice more in the back. In other words, he is riddled with bullets front and back at a moment when there is no longer an ongoing attack. However, unlike the woman in Mükemmel Komşu ("The Perfect Neighbor"), the father who shot James is found not guilty.

The woman in The Perfect Neighbor:

1. Was home alone, whereas in the James case, there were two other people in the house.
2. Was not a strong, able-bodied man like the father who shot James.
3. Had her door being constantly pounded, indicating an ongoing attack, and she had no knowledge of whether the person at her door had a weapon or a similar object; they had previously thrown signs, skateboards, etc., at each other.
4. First called the police, saying, "They are at my door, I'm very scared." In fact, in her subsequent statement, she testified, "I was afraid you wouldn't come again, as there were previous situations where I called and you didn't come."
5. Two minutes after this call, she fires a single, blind shot through the door, killing the Black woman.

"Stand Your Ground" and the "Castle Doctrine" have critical details, such as the requirement that the attack must be ongoing and the absence of an intent for revenge. The reason the woman received a 25-year sentence was the conclusion that she acted with the intent of revenge.

However, it seems that when the father who pulled the trigger in the James case fired multiple shots through the door at his daughter's boyfriend, whom he knew had been harming his daughter for a long time, he is not considered to have acted with the intent of revenge. Yet, the woman in The Perfect Neighbor, who got into a verbal argument with the neighborhood kids, is deemed to have killed their mother with the intent of revenge over a trivial matter :)

On the other hand, the fact that the woman in The Perfect Neighbor first called the police for help and then fired a single shot through the door two minutes later supports the idea that she felt she had "no other choice." In the James case, however, the father continuing to fire at the fleeing James in the back, after the attack had ended, transforms the action from "defense" into an act of "ensuring absolute death."

In the documentary, I expected evidence to be presented showing that the woman had studied the aforementioned self-defense principles in detail on her phone or home computer and had planned to use the gun with a scenario that would justify her actions. However, there was no information in this direction.

The prosecution had to prove that the woman committed this act not as "self-defense" but as a premeditated act of "revenge" or "punishment." To support this claim, evidence should have been presented that the woman had researched "self-defense" laws in detail on her phone or computer before the incident or had planned to use the gun. Although the documentary provided no information about the existence of such evidence, through commentary, the impression was created that she acted with such intent.

In conclusion, in those distant lands where these events took place, it is not the rules of law but jury dynamics and societal prejudices that completely steer the trials. In the James case, there was the figure of a "heroic father" who killed someone he perceived as a threat to his property, saving his precious daughter from the wrath of an angry boyfriend, and he was set free. In contrast, the woman in The Perfect Neighbor was a "grumpy single woman" who killed a "mother of four" and received a 25-year sentence. Thus, it is clear and obvious to what extent the sympathy and antipathy created by the media influence the courts.

comment_text:
For all the jurors and judges commenting on this: you can’t know what someone is thinking or what’s in their heart, so let’s leave that up to God.  In my opinion only, it seemed that Susan was extremely mentally unstable.  The way she rammed the fence and damaged her nice truck and the gate is not something an emotionally stable person would do if they were afraid they were locked in.  Her rants about the children may have rightly caused her neighbors to be resentful.   

However, her neighbors should have viewed her as a mentally unstable woman (like I believe she was) and told their kids to stay far, far away from her.  Instead, it appears they thought her rants were racially motivated, and they pushed the boundaries.  

There are no winners in this case.  Those poor children lost what appeared to be a wonderful mother, and a woman who was already struggling mentally and emotionally has more trauma to deal with.  It’s sad that Netflix appears to be making this about race, instead of about a paranoid and mentally disturbed woman who wrongly killed her neighbor.

video_title: The Perfect Neighbor | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
I did not find this documentary profound or emotional at all. It left me deeply disappointed in a flawed system and a crazy world. I am by no means condoning what Susan did — it was absolutely horrific, and it’s devastating that four children have lost their mother. But having said that, housing situations and neighbourhood disputes are among the most stressful things a person can experience. We are only seeing what the documentary wanted to show, and even that created a narrative in which Susan was always going to come off as the villain. To simply write this off as “kids being kids” and suggest they should roam free, terrorising the neighbourhood, is absurd.

That road was rough. You could tell just by looking at the adults being interviewed: women clearly under the influence — one even had a beer in her hand and looked like she was on meth — and a man who was stoned. All the rubbish on the floor, with kids seeing this and being enabled by parents who clearly suffered from Peter Pan syndrome, all over the question of “ownership of a road.” Complaining that Susan didn’t own the land misses the point — she had rights, and one of them was to live peacefully and not be a persistent victim of crime. Those kids, without a shadow of a doubt, were tormenting her, and I believe even the parents and friends of the parents were complicit. There’s nothing more stressful than children behaving like menaces; it can absolutely push a person to the edge.

I don’t even want to get started on the police officers, who failed in their duty of care to a protected characteristic such as Susan. Her first report should have triggered an investigation, and these issues should have been reported to child protective services and the landlord. There should have been an ASB contract in place with clear dos and don’ts. They could have flagged the road as a high-crime area and conducted regular patrols. Instead, they did nothing but enable the problematic neighbours and mock Susan.

Susan had a completely different mindset from the other neighbours. She was built differently. I have no doubt she feared being jumped — she probably thought more people were coming for her, or perhaps that they had weapons; we don’t know, as we only see the incidents the documentary showed. I believe someone can be pushed to the edge, and I believe she fired the gun thinking it would just show she wasn’t going to tolerate their behaviour. I don’t see her as a murderer, but she does have to live with the consequences of her actions.

video_title: The Perfect Neighbor | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: When I was a child, there was an elderly woman who lived alone, and our shouting and play disturbed her. At first, my mother and the other children's mothers defended us and accused the woman of being bitter because she didn’t have children of her own and therefore hated other people’s kids. That gave us, the children, a sense of encouragement—we became even louder, played right in front of her door, and picked her flowers. One neighbor stood up for the woman and spoke with our parents. They realized their children weren’t exactly angels and began to correct us. We found another place to play, stopped picking her flowers, and as we grew up, we actually developed a good relationship with that woman.
